comments.author: Steve
comments.text: yup, simple and self-evident when you put it like that

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
This type of reasoning is the kind of reasoning we need to use whenever we put on our "data scientist" hats.  We have to manufacture monotony at times. Because monotony is part of life.

But it's usually the opposite!  What made the mouse-slip test unrealistic was the monotony itself.  We couldn't separate out the monotony complication without introducing other complications for collecting results.  The best we could do was give me a break after every 10 tasks.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
I'm glad you brought it back to programming. I think programming mistakes happen under both!!!

Concentration is the resource. Pressure works against concentration in the immediate time scale--death by the gun to my head.  Boredom works against concentration over the duration--death by a thousand paper cuts.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: It's actually more about the pressure!! Actually, it's more about just having to task-switch. The computer analogy here is pretty obvious. Small working memory. Task switches are very expensive.
